chunks.sourceTitle: BMW Group
chunks.heading: Lead Frontend Engineer
chunks.content: BMW Group (2018–2019) — Lead Frontend Engineer. Innovation platforms for the BMW Group — strategic R&D challenges across future mobility, sustainability, connected vehicles. Patterns: Enterprise scale.

chunks.sourceTitle: Deloitte
chunks.heading: Software Engineer
chunks.content: Deloitte (2016–2018) — Software Engineer. Enterprise data-visualization surfaces for clients in financial services and regulated industries. Patterns: Enterprise scale.

chunks.sourceTitle: Diligent
chunks.heading: Lead Frontend Engineer
chunks.content: Diligent (2019–2020) — Lead Frontend Engineer. Authored the company-wide React + Angular enterprise design system serving Fortune-1000-class governance products. Patterns: Design-system infrastructure, Enterprise scale.

chunks.sourceTitle: eino.ai
chunks.heading: Lead Frontend Engineer
chunks.content: eino.ai (2023–2025) — Lead Frontend Engineer. Agentic RF network planning, digital-twin maps, real-time heatmaps. Shipped agentic UX ahead of industry adoption. Patterns: AI-native platforms, Agentic UX.

chunks.sourceTitle: Fueled
chunks.heading: Lead Engineer, Web Applications
chunks.content: Fueled (2025+) — Lead Engineer, Web Applications. Frontend architecture for enterprise web platforms across AI, media, and digital-transformation engagements. Patterns: AI-native platforms, Design-system infrastructure, Enterprise scale.

chunks.sourceTitle: Moment
chunks.heading: VP of Engineering
chunks.content: Moment (2025) — VP of Engineering. Took an AI-native knowledge platform from prototype velocity to production reliability. Owned hiring, leveling, RFCs, on-call. Patterns: AI-native platforms, Agentic UX.

chunks.sourceTitle: Sky · NBCUniversal · Peacock
chunks.heading: Senior Software Engineer
chunks.content: Sky · NBCUniversal · Peacock (2020–2022) — Senior Software Engineer. Tens-of-millions-of-viewers scale on streaming + commerce surfaces. Reliability, latency, release safety on the surfaces where regressions hit minutes after deploy. Patterns: Streaming-grade reliability, Enterprise scale.

chunks.sourceTitle: An enterprise design system that survived two frameworks
chunks.heading: Summary
chunks.content: An enterprise design system that survived two frameworks. Lead frontend engineer on Diligent's company-wide enterprise design system — multi-framework, governed, contributed-to by product teams, used across the company's governance suite serving Fortune-1000 customers.

chunks.sourceTitle: An enterprise design system that survived two frameworks
chunks.content:
Diligent ships governance software to Fortune-1000-class boards, executives, and audit committees. The product surface is conservative, regulated, and serious — exactly the constituency that benefits from a design system, and exactly the constituency that punishes a bad one.

I led the frontend side of Diligent's company-wide design system in 2019–2020. The interesting parts of this case study are not "we shipped Buttons and Modals." Every enterprise DS does. The interesting parts are how the system survived running on two frameworks, and how contribution got federated without losing coherence.

chunks.sourceTitle: An enterprise design system that survived two frameworks
chunks.heading: The brief, in one paragraph
chunks.content:
## The brief, in one paragraph

Diligent had bought and built product lines across years; the suite spoke with several mildly different voices. The new design system was asked to do three things at once: unify the voice without a rewrite, let two framework communities consume it without forking, and create a path for product teams to contribute components without breaking the center.

That third one is the hard one. Most enterprise design systems die not from missing components but from a contribution model that can't keep up with product teams' velocity.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai
chunks.heading: Summary
chunks.content: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai. Lead engineer on an agentic platform that compresses weeks of RF network planning into hours. Digital-twin maps, agent orchestration, human-in-the-loop review — production agentic UX before the category existed.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai
chunks.content:
This is a case study about building agentic UX in production — not a demo, not a hackathon. eino.ai's product compresses what a senior RF engineer does in a week into a working session: ingest a customer brief, draft sites, plan radio links, validate against terrain + line-of-sight, and hand the engineer a high-level design they can sign off on or revise.

My remit was the whole client side: an agent-aware React + GraphQL foundation, a digital-twin map surface, the human-in-the-loop review loop that keeps the agent honest, and a contract with the backend crisp enough to absorb model swaps without UI rewrites.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai
chunks.heading: Architecture
chunks.content:
## Architecture

The orchestrator is a thin GraphQL gateway in front of the agents. The frontend treats every leg as a streamed mutation: the engineer triggers a leg, the leg streams partial proposals, and the map renders them as they arrive. The agents themselves are out of scope for this writeup — I owned the surface and the contract.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai
chunks.heading: The product, in one paragraph
chunks.content:
## The product, in one paragraph

Customers come in with a vague brief: "connect these N rural clusters." The agent reads the brief, drafts candidate radio sites on a real-world map, runs line-of-sight against terrain, proposes backhaul links, fits the radios, and hands the engineer a high-level network design they can edit. Editing anywhere in that chain re-triggers the affected agents — the plan is a graph, not a wizard.

What makes it agentic, not "AI-flavored," is that every step is a discrete tool call the engineer can inspect, rerun, or override. The UI has to expose that without overwhelming the engineer with logs.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic RF network planning at eino.ai
chunks.heading: What this looked like to the engineer
chunks.content:
## What this looked like to the engineer

The hero of the product is the map. The agents are the supporting cast. That distinction shaped every UI decision: the map was the surface that held attention; agent panels were edges of the workspace that lit up when something needed acceptance.

chunks.sourceTitle: Streaming-grade reliability at Peacock
chunks.heading: Summary
chunks.content: Streaming-grade reliability at Peacock. Senior engineer on the Peacock web surfaces during launch and scale-up. The work was less about features and more about not breaking on a minute-by-minute basis — release safety, performance, and the discipline a global streaming surface demands.

chunks.sourceTitle: Streaming-grade reliability at Peacock
chunks.content:
The honest framing of this one: I was not a system architect at Peacock. I was a senior engineer on web surfaces during a period when "don't be the reason it broke" mattered more than "ship the clever idea." The case study is about the working habits that produced quietly reliable code at that scale.

If you're hiring me for streaming-grade work, this is the engagement where I learned what the words streaming-grade actually mean.

chunks.sourceTitle: Streaming-grade reliability at Peacock
chunks.heading: What the surface actually was
chunks.content:
## What the surface actually was

Peacock's web property is the non-player half of the streaming experience: auth, sign-up, billing, account, content browse, error recovery, deep-linking from native apps. None of it is glamorous in isolation; in aggregate it's the surface that decides whether a viewer ever reaches the player on Sunday afternoon.

The work was less "design a system" and more "operate a system that already exists at scale, without making it worse." This was where I internalized that for senior engineers in operating-scale companies, the unsexy parts — release safety, observability, runbooks — are the craft.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: Summary
chunks.content: Agentic UX without the demo tax. Most AI-flavored interfaces are still demos. Production agentic UX is the discipline of treating LLMs like flaky tools the user can supervise — not magic the user has to trust.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.content:
Most "AI" interfaces I see in 2025 are demos with a router. They ship a chat surface, attach a tool-use loop, and call it agentic. They look impressive in a 90-second sales call. They become useless in hour two of the engineer's actual workday.

This is what I mean by the demo tax: the cost of building a product whose primary success metric is its ability to be demoed, rather than its ability to be lived in. You can ship a lot of demos before you notice you're not shipping a product.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: The framing I keep coming back to
chunks.content:
## The framing I keep coming back to

Anything an agent does has to be inspectable, rerunnable, and attributable. The UI can be magical; the contract underneath cannot.

Every agentic surface I've seen succeed in production has obeyed that rule. Most of the demos I see weekly are still operating on the opposite assumption — that the user will hand-wave at the agent's output if the loading animation is sufficiently impressive.

If you're building one of these surfaces, the test is straightforward: can a skeptical engineer use the product for a full day without losing trust in it? If yes, you have a product. If no, you have a demo. There's no middle category, and there's no time-on-the-market shortcut around the difference.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: What production agentic UX actually requires
chunks.content:
## What production agentic UX actually requires

I've shipped one production agentic system at this point — eino.ai — and the lessons cluster into a small number of obvious-in-hindsight rules that almost no demo I've seen follows.

1. Agents are streaming mutations, not chat turns

If you treat an agent run as a chat turn, the user sits on a spinner for 30 seconds and then gets a wall of output. That's the demo pattern. It's the same pattern as a slow API call with a personality.

The production pattern is to stream partial proposals back. The user sees the agent working — site by site, link by link, line by line — and the surface updates in place. By the time the agent finishes, the user has been with it for the run, not waiting for it.

This isn't a UI flourish. It's a contract decision: every agent leg becomes a streamed mutation with a stable ID and a sequence number, and the UI is built to reconcile partials. Doing this later, after the demo ships, is a rewrite. Doing it first is a week.

2. Every agent leg has to be inspectable

The product engineer's first question, when they sit down with an agent, is what did it do and why. If the answer is "I'll trust it," you've shipped a chatbot. If the answer is "click into the leg, see the inputs, see the outputs, see the model + prompt version, rerun with different inputs," you've shipped an agent.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: What production agentic UX actually requires
chunks.content:
## What production agentic UX actually requires

The cost of building this is real and unavoidable. The cost of not building it is that engineers stop using the product after their second false-positive.

3. The user isn't the prompt; the user is the editor

A persistent failure mode I see is treating the human as a prompt input. They type something, the agent runs, output appears, repeat. This is the chat metaphor. It scales to "ask a vague question, get a vague answer."

Production agentic UX flips this. The human is the editor of the plan. The agent is one of several proposers. The plan is the durable artifact — not a transcript, not a chat history. The user edits the plan; the agent reproposes around the edits. The chat, if it exists at all, is a side channel.

This shift — from chat to editor — is the single biggest UX delta between the demos and the production systems I trust.

4. The contract has to outlive the model

LLMs change. Prompts change. Pipelines get rewritten. If your UI knows what model is running, what version of what prompt is firing, or how the agent steps are composed, your UI is a brittle dependency of the agent team.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: What production agentic UX actually requires
chunks.content:
## What production agentic UX actually requires

The contract that should be visible to the UI is what the agent returns (a typed proposal, a typed diff, a typed cost estimate) — not how it computed it. We swapped LLM providers three times at eino.ai; the UI shipped through every swap without a single visible regression to pilot customers. The contract was the spine; the model was an implementation detail.

5. Recovery is a feature, not an error state

Demos elide failure. Production agentic surfaces have to elevate it. Every leg must be:

- Rewindable: if the proposal was wrong, the user can roll back to before it ran without losing other state.
- Rerunnable: with the same inputs (for debugging) or with new ones (for iteration).
- Diffable: if the user reran a leg, they should be able to see what changed and choose which version to keep.

If failure recovery is "click the back button and try again," you've built a search engine, not an agent.

chunks.sourceTitle: Agentic UX without the demo tax
chunks.heading: What this looks like in practice
chunks.content:
## What this looks like in practice

The architecture isn't complicated. The discipline is.

For most teams shipping agentic features in 2025, the question isn't "can we get the LLM to do the thing." That part is solved. The question is whether the UI around it is honest enough about the LLM's failure modes that engineers stop dropping out after the demo.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: Summary
chunks.content: Design systems that survive multiple product lines. What separates a design system that survives a five-year, multi-product engagement from one that ships eight Buttons and dies. Hint: it isn't the components.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.content:
I have been the lead frontend engineer on design systems at three companies. The first one outlived me. The second one is the one I think about most when I write this. The third one is still in flight.

Here's what I've learned about the systems that survive — not the ones that launch with a fancy Storybook, but the ones that are still used three product cycles later — by separating the wheat from the chaff.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: A note on metrics
chunks.content:
## A note on metrics

People love DS adoption metrics. "70% of product surfaces use the Modal component." Fine. They're not what a senior reviewer should be looking at.

The metrics that matter are operational:

- Contribution velocity: how often does someone outside the DS team contribute back? Once a quarter is a warning. Once a sprint is a healthy system.
- Token coverage (not component coverage): how many non-library products are pulling the tokens? This shows the actual reach of the design language.
- Contract churn: how often does a component's contract change? Frequent churn means the contract was wrong; rare churn means it was right.

Adoption rate is a vanity metric. Contribution rate is the real one.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: The actual work, in order
chunks.content:
## The actual work, in order

1. Tokens come first, and they are the product

Colors, type, spacing, motion, radius — declared once, in a runtime-agnostic format (JSON is fine; we don't need a magic format). This is the contract. Components are one consumer of it; products that won't adopt the component library can still consume the tokens and look like the rest of the suite.

Tokens being the product means a design language can spread further than your library can. That's a feature.

2. A contract is written before code

For every component, write a doc: props, slots, ARIA, focus behavior, motion, edge cases. Both the React and the Angular team (or Vue, or whatever) implement that doc. The doc, not either implementation, is the canonical artifact.

Contract-first sounds slow. The first six weeks ship very few components. After that it's faster than the alternative — and the divergence problem doesn't exist.

3. The contribution model is the system

This is the part teams miss. If a product team has a high-quality Banner they need next week, the system either lets them contribute it back or it loses adoption. Both options are choices; one is a strategy and one is an accident.

A working contribution model has, at minimum:

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: The actual work, in order
chunks.content:
## The actual work, in order

- An RFC template. Two paragraphs is fine; the point is to think through it before code.
- A design review with the design system's owners.
- A code review focused on the contract, not the bikeshed.
- An a11y review (if your products are regulated, this is the most critical of the four).
- A docs + Storybook entry.

The central team doesn't write the component. They review it. That's the difference between gatekeeping and federation.

4. Accessibility is in the contract, not in the docs

A11y checklists in the docs are the design-system equivalent of "please be safe out there." They are well-meaning, and they accomplish nothing.

The way a11y survives the lifecycle of a system is by being a contract behavior: a Button without an accessible label fails TypeScript. A Modal without focus management fails review. A toggle without proper roles isn't accepted into the system. The contract is the enforcement.

5. Tokens, components, contribution, adoption — in that order

Most systems do this in reverse. They ship 30 components first, then write tokens, then try to set up contribution, then beg for adoption. By the time they get to contribution, the system has accumulated enough opinions that no contribution can fit it.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: The failure mode is never components
chunks.content:
## The failure mode is never components

If you go on the internet looking for advice about enterprise design systems, almost all of it is about components: what variants the Button has, whether the Modal traps focus, whether the Tabs use role="tablist". This is fine, but it isn't the work. Every enterprise DS I've seen ships those.

The systems that die die at one of three places:

If you can name a design system that died, I can almost certainly trace it back to one of these three.

chunks.sourceTitle: Design systems that survive multiple product lines
chunks.heading: The framing I keep coming back to
chunks.content:
## The framing I keep coming back to

A design system is not a component library. It's a contract for how products in this organization speak to their users.

That framing changes what work matters. Component sprints become a side-effect of contract sprints. Adoption strategy becomes "make the system the obvious tool" instead of "mandate the system." And contribution becomes the central activity, not an afterthought.

If you're hiring a Staff+ engineer to own a design system, the work they should describe — when you ask them about their last DS engagement — is contribution, contract, and federation. If they describe Buttons and Modals, they are at a more junior altitude than the role calls for. That's the test.

chunks.sourceTitle: Diogo Esteves
chunks.heading: Identity
chunks.content: Diogo Esteves — Staff / Principal Frontend & Platform Engineer. AI-native systems, enterprise UI infrastructure, scalable web architectures. Based in Lisbon, Portugal · Remote (US-aligned hours). Open to Staff+, Principal, Founding Engineer, and VP / Head of Engineering roles. Operating companies: Fueled, Moment, eino.ai, NBCUniversal · Peacock, Diligent, BMW Group, Deloitte.
